Investigating the word length effect on short term memory

Buchananâs experiment

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear and explicit aim with explanation of relevance to the phonological loop and educational strategies
Accurate and well-connected description of the Working Memory Model
Operationalised independent and dependent variables with both directional and null hypotheses
weaknesses: Minor delay in stating the investigation aim within the introduction
Typographical error in spelling âBaddeleyâ reduces academic precision
Educational rationale is concise and could be elaborated for fuller context
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two of three introduction strands fully meet the 5â6 band descriptors and the hypothesis strand fully satisfies the top band. Minor issues in presentation and brevity prevent a perfect 6, so a 5 reflects high-quality work with small room for improvement.

strengths: Research design is not only described but also accompanied by a clear rationale for counterbalancing
Sampling technique is identified with basic demographic justification
Materials and controlled variables are clearly described and reasons for controls are provided
weaknesses: Limited reflection on how convenience sampling affects generalisability
Insufficient detail on randomisation procedures for word lists
Explanation of design benefits is solid but could be extended to fully articulate all advantages
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands achieve the âexplainedâ level (band 3â4) while sampling remains at a solid âdescribedâ level (band 1â2). Overall performance aligns with a mid-band score of 3 out of 4.

strengths: Statistical findings are clearly linked to the hypothesis and the null is explicitly rejected
Interpretation of results discusses significance with regard to the research question
weaknesses: Use of Mann-Whitney U is inappropriate for a repeated-measures design
Graphs lack proper axis labels and contain annotation errors
Inconsistency in reported statistics (e.g., misprinted mean value) detracts from accuracy
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While interpretation is strong, fundamental errors in test selection and graphical presentation place two of three strands in the 3â4 band at the lower end. A score of 3 reflects solid interpretative skill but significant methodological flaws.

strengths: Discussion effectively references the Working Memory Model and prior research to contextualise findings
Strengths and limitations are relevant, explained, and tied to validity considerations
Modifications are explicitly linked to identified limitations and are well justified
weaknesses: Depth of theoretical discussion is somewhat superficial in places
Evaluation section ends mid-paragraph, indicating incomplete exposition
Conclusion could more fully connect to wider implications beyond the immediate study
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three evaluation strands satisfy the 5â6 band descriptors with clear linkage and justification. Minor depth and completeness issues prevent a perfect 6, so a 5 accurately reflects performance.

strandSelections.reasoning: Two graphs (boxplots and bar chart) that speak directly to the hypothesis are included. Yet the bar chart lacks axis labels and the boxplots are not fully annotated (title repetition, unclear units). These execution flaws mean the work âaddresses the hypothesis but contains errorsâ, placing it in the 3-4 band with a mid score.

The student clearly states that the mean recall was higher for monosyllabic words, cites the significant p-value, explicitly rejects the null, and links the outcome back to the hypothesis. Interpretation is sound; the only minor weakness is an imprecise statement that data were âsignificantly largerâ without effect size. Nonetheless this comfortably fulfils the 5-6 band.

Both IV (number of syllables: mono vs penta) and DV (number of words correctly recalled in order) are clearly stated and operationalised. Directional (research) and null hypotheses are written using those operational definitions. This meets the top-band requirement without identifiable omissions, warranting full marks in the band.

Convenience sampling is identified, basic demographic details (age, schooling, language) are provided, and reasons of feasibility and linguistic homogeneity are given. While the rationale is present, discussion of representativeness or alternative techniques is limited, so mid-band (3) is a balanced allocation.

The Working Memory Model is outlined, key components (central executive, phonological loop, articulatory control) are described, and the function of the phonological loop is linked directly to the predicted word-length effect. The description is accurate and the connection to the planned investigation is explicitly articulated, fulfilling the 5-6 band.

Findings are related to Baddeley et al. and to the phonological loop, with discussion of alignment and discrepancies. While the theoretical discussion is not exhaustive, it moves well beyond mere description, satisfying the top band; a 5 (not 6) reflects some superficiality in depth.

The aim â to examine whether word length (monosyllabic vs pentasyllabic) affects serial-recall accuracy â is explicitly stated. The student goes on to explain why this matters for understanding the phonological loop of the Working Memory Model and even for educational note-taking strategies. Because both the aim and an explicit explanation of relevance are present, performance matches the top band. Minor brevity in the educational rationale prevents awarding the very top of the band.

Multiple strengths (familiarisation period, repeated measures) and limitations (small sample, spelling rule, paper contamination) are identified and each is explained in terms of its impact on validity or generalisability. The explanations are coherent though not highly detailed, warranting a solid but not perfect score in the top band.

Materials (PowerPoint lists, recordings, timed intervals) and several controls (familiarisation period, counterbalancing, 1.5-s word spacing) are listed and the reasons for them are explained (e.g., controlling familiarity, order effects). This fits the âexplainedâ descriptor, so the upper value of the 3-4 band has been awarded.

Proposed changes (independent-samples design, larger and more diverse sample, digital response collection) are explicitly tied to previously stated limitations and are rationally justified. This meets the descriptor for full justification; depth is acceptable though not exceptional, so 5 is appropriate within the 5-6 band.

The student not only describes using a repeated-measures design but also explains why it reduces participant variability and identifies potential order effects that were counterbalanced. This level of justification constitutes an explanation rather than a simple description, placing it in the 3-4 band; the explanation is clear so the top of that band is appropriate.

Descriptive measures and an inferential test (Mann-Whitney U) are provided. However, the Mann-Whitney is inappropriate for a repeated-measures design; therefore statistics are âapplied but contain errorsâ. This aligns with the 3-4 band, and a lower score within that band is justified by the fundamental test-choice error.

annotations.comment: The title succinctly describes the focus on the word length effect and indicates the replication context, establishing a clear research scope.
The table of contents clearly outlines the structure but would benefit from including page numbers for each section to improve navigability.
The materials and procedure are well controlled with familiarization and timing, showing thoughtful operational planning.
The introductory paragraph provides rich background on cognitive mechanisms but delays the statement of the investigation aim; consider introducing the study objective earlier.
The integration of Milnerâs (1965) STM limitations with the phonological loop concept effectively frames the theoretical justification for investigating the word length effect.
Baddeley is misspelled as âBaddleyâ in the text; ensure accurate spelling of key theoristsâ names to maintain academic precision.
The replication aim is clearly articulated and linked to both theoretical validation and practical educational strategies, fulfilling the introduction requirements.
Independent and dependent variables are clearly operationalized in the hypotheses, aligning well with the investigation design.
H0 is stated as non-directional; consider clarifying whether you expect equal recall or a directional change to improve hypothesis precision.
The practical implication sentence on adapting educational strategies would be more impactful if positioned after a full explanation of relevance in the introduction.
The explanation of the repeated measures design notes reduced variability but ends mid-sentence; extend the rationale to fully articulate benefits and counterbalancing.
Convenience sampling and participant demographics are described, but further reflection on how this affects representativeness would strengthen the exploration.
Consider specifying how randomization of word lists was implemented (e.g., software or algorithm) to fully explain variable control.
The control of order effects via counterbalancing and pilot testing is wellâjustified, demonstrating robust experimental planning.
Include a descriptive title and clarify units in Table 1 to improve readability and ensure clear data interpretation.
The boxplot is appropriate for comparing conditions but lacks axis labels; ensure axes are labeled with units and variable names.
Interpretation references mean recall in a boxplot context, but boxplots visualize medians and IQR; revise to discuss the median rather than the mean.
The bar chart effectively compares means but omits axis labels and numerical values on bars; add these for precision.
The stated mean for pentasyllabic recall is misprinted as M=2.18 instead of 3.07; double-check reported statistics against Table 1.
Mann-Whitney U is inappropriate for repeated-measures data; consider using the Wilcoxon signed-ranks test to correctly analyze paired observations.
The discussion of statistical findings clearly rejects Hâ and links results to the hypothesis, demonstrating accurate interpretation.
Consider reporting an effect size (e.g., r or Cohenâs d) to provide practical significance alongside the p-value.
The discussion references original research and contrasts methodology, reflecting solid engagement with background theory.
Limitations are linked to validity but the discussion prematurely ends mid-paragraph; ensure complete exposition for clarity.
The proposed shift to an independent-samples design is logical, but the trade-off with control should be explicitly linked to the identified order effects.
The limitation on spelling errors is well-articulated, demonstrating understanding of the phonological loopâs reliance on sound over orthography.
To fully justify the digital response modification, explicitly tie it to the response contamination limitation and explain how privacy will be ensured.
The brief conclusion summarizes the main finding effectively, though adding a sentence linking back to wider implications would enhance closure.
